550cea39_text: Hey there. Before we get started today, I wanted to take a few minutes[br4.492][br4.573]and talk through dosage calculation and medication errors.[br8.04][br8.121]Many of the students I work with worry a lot about making mistakes and how to respond to them.[br13.094][br13.175]What approach do you usually take when it comes to errors?[br16.151]
29e4d969_title: Unless it's an emergency, try to fix the error, then get help.
a6804283_text: Taking care of errors on your own is a helpful instinct. It's also important to remember the rest of the team is there to support you, even and especially when an error has been made.
4adfaf64_title: Tell a supervisor about it immediately.
a476101f_text: Supervisors are always available to support you, especially when an error has been made. In some cases, it can be worth trying to resolve the error on your own first, and then go to a supervisor.
ede0959f_title: Focus on avoiding mistakes in the first place.
380d1b9b_text: Prevention is almost always the best approach. However, sometimes errors do happen, and then it's important to address the error, whether by yourself or by informing someone else, depending on the error.
39f516c3_text: Keep your approach to errors in mind as we talk through a situation from a couple years back,[br5.589][br5.67]when I was supervising a student named Vic in clinicals.[br9.449][br9.53]He was taking care of a client, Rhea, who had just undergone a cholecystectomy.[br14.216]
e95ce064_text: Rhea had nothing by mouth, also known as NPO, before her procedure.[br5.443][br5.524]After the cholecystectomy, Rhea returned to her room on the surgical unit[br9.526][br9.607]in stable condition with her pain level under control.[br13.066][br13.147]Vic briefly checked the electronic health record, or EHR,[br17.16][br17.241]and saw postop orders that indicated Rhea was placed on a clear liquid diet,[br21.851][br21.932]and there was an order for her to receive 3 units of insulin subcutaneously.[br27.34][br27.421]If you were Vic, what would you do in this situation?[br30.813]
c495c6e4_text: Not right away. We don't have enough information to immediately administer the insulin.[br5.69]
fee1ecb8_title: Recheck the orders to make sure nothing was missed.
de0a7190_text: [br0.015]That's right. Surgeons complete postop orders that usually include[br4.413][br4.494]all the medications, procedures, and care needed after surgery.[br8.416][br8.562]In this case with a client who is NPO, we want to make sure[br11.888][br11.969]insulin is being administered exactly as outlined in the orders.[br15.578]
684be09b_text: Correct. When clients are NPO, they often are given IV fluids, which can contain glucose,[br6.286][br6.367]but before administering insulin, we should always check a client's blood glucose level[br10.656][br10.737]to make sure the dose is necessary and appropriate.[br13.975]
681b3c4b_title: Make sure Rhea has received previous insulin doses.
c19e556e_text: Although this is something that should be in the EHR, it doesn't guide whether to give insulin or not.[br6.671][br6.752]Postop blood glucose levels can vary greatly based on physiological stress and nutritional intake.[br13.064]
19329c85_title: Those are all the actions I would take at this time.
a5b13761_text: What else, if anything, would you do?[br2.467]
29f31cef_text: In general, administering insulin without knowing blood glucose levels[br4.388][br4.469]and double-checking the EHR can lead to medication errors.[br8.448][br8.529]Even after carefully checking the EHR, it's important to consider all possible information and cues,[br15.154][br15.235]like blood glucose level, because there could be a system error if Rhea's postop orders were not updated properly.[br22.843][br22.924]In this case, Vic followed what he saw in the EHR and administered the 3 units of insulin.[br29.113][br29.194]This is an example of an individual error.[br31.928]
9bc14692_text: When I checked on Rhea just a few minutes later, I found her confused and agitated.[br5.681][br5.762]She was sweating profusely and seemed lethargic. What do these cues suggest was most likely happening with Rhea?[br13.048]
c5b89f15_title: Rhea could be internally bleeding from the cholecystectomy.
bf668f24_text: I wouldn't think so. Internal bleeding does often manifest with sweating and lethargy,[br5.539][br5.62]but agitation and confusion are less common.[br9.007]
3a22d6db_title: Rhea might be going into sepsis from an infection we missed.
06631082_text: Sepsis feels unlikely in this scenario. Sweating, lethargy, and confusion are[br5.206][br5.287]manifestations of sepsis, but the agitation doesn't match.[br9.511]
03886b49_title: Rhea is probably hypoglycemic because she received insulin.
e47b97f9_text: That seems like the most likely hypothesis to me.[br3.689][br3.77]If Rhea's blood glucose was low and then she received insulin, she could easily become hypoglycemic.[br11.05]
b2b396d3_title: It's possible Rhea is having an allergic reaction.
af97943f_text: I wouldn't think Rhea was having an allergic reaction.[br2.944][br3.025]Allergic reactions can cause agitation, but the lethargy, sweating, and confusion don't add up.[br9.105]
8074805e_text: Since we know Vic administered insulin after Rhea was NPO, she's more likely hypoglycemic.[br6.301]
0823f37b_text: What would you do first if you suspected your client was hypoglycemic?[br4.777]
4888db63_text: Not quite. While we will very likely want to give Rhea clear liquid carbs like apple juice,[br6.024][br6.105]we don't want to do anything until we've checked the blood glucose levels.[br10.143]
1448ef98_text: That's correct. We want to know Rhea's glucose levels before we do anything else.[br5.642]
fc406d04_text: If we suspect a client might be hypoglycemic, the last thing we want to do is administer more insulin.[br6.561][br6.642]While we will very likely want to give Rhea clear liquid carbs like apple juice,[br11.033][br11.289]we don't want to do anything until we've checked the blood glucose levels.[br15.294]
194ca322_text: Although it is important to monitor postoperative vital signs, in this moment,[br5.824][br5.905]the blood pressure is not as important as knowing the blood glucose levels and possibly giving carbohydrates.[br12.167]
cee68dcb_text: If Rhea was still NPO, we might need to reach out to the provider for an order for D50W.[br6.98][br7.061]In this case, Rhea's blood glucose level was 51 mg/dL,[br12.115][br12.196]so we decided to give her apple juice, as she was on a clear liquid diet.[br16.429][br16.51]Then, we'd monitor and assess Rhea over the course of about 15 minutes[br21.119][br21.2]and check her glucose levels again and determine the next steps.[br25.199][br25.28]At the same time, we wanted to let Rhea know what was going on. How would you explain the situation to her?[br31.944]
c56eab55_title: "Vic gave you insulin when he shouldn't have, so now we need to stabilize your glucose level."
372fd98e_text: In the moment, we want to focus on stabilizing the client.[br4.07][br4.151]After the situation is resolved, we can go into more detail about the drop in blood glucose,[br9.299][br9.38]but even then, we don't want to assign blame to Vic or anyone else on the health care team.[br15.204]
43694621_title: "Don't panic, but you're hypoglycemic. If we can't get your blood glucose levels up, you really get into trouble."
4b14e1b8_text: Using scare tactics with clients is never the right approach because it might induce panic,[br5.381][br5.462]which can negatively impact their health outcomes. Instead, stick to the most relevant facts.[br11.619]
85be6141_title: "Your blood glucose level is low, but don't worry about it. Everything will be fine."
3f1a91ca_text: Giving false reassurance to clients is not an effective approach because it can mislead them into thinking[br5.785][br5.866]everything is fine when we don't know that yet. Instead, stick to the most relevant facts.[br11.579]
d6899848_title: "Your blood glucose level is low, so we're giving you some carbohydrates to get it back up."
484b5fda_text: We want to stick to the facts and be honest with Rhea about what the problem is and how we're addressing it.[br6.218][br6.299]Fifteen minutes later, Rhea's blood glucose level rose to 80 mg/dL, which was back inside of a safe range.[br13.937]
60a68364_text: Once Rhea started to stabilize, we needed to report and document the error[br4.833][br4.914]and potentially also communicate with the provider or other people who are relevant because Vic was a student.[br10.826][br10.907]For details on how to report and document errors,[br13.802][br13.883]familiarize yourself with the policies and procedures of the facility where you work about incident or variance reports.[br20.063]
448eccf0_text: In our case, we needed to update the EHR with information about the drop in blood glucose,[br5.731][br5.812]the clear liquid carbohydrates, and how the blood glucose level changed afterward.[br10.713][br10.794]We also needed to directly contact Rhea's primary care provider and surgeon[br15.187][br15.268]to make sure they were aware of the incident.[br17.782][br17.863]Finally, we made sure to communicate to the next shift what happened so they could monitor Rhea as needed.[br23.272]
045c1263_text: Afterward, when we had a calmer moment, I talked with Vic[br4.057][br4.138]and gave him some feedback on how to avoid errors like this in the future.[br8.34][br8.421]If you were in my position, what instruction would you give Vic?[br12.114]
2d476f80_text: This isn't the best advice. We should always be cautious and remain aware of the potential for human error,[br5.968][br6.049]but the EHR should be considered a helpful tool.[br9.185][br9.704]Instead, we should remind Vic to double-check orders in the EHR,[br14.214][br14.295]especially if they seem like they could be risky for the client.[br17.605][br17.686]If need be, orders can always be confirmed with the provider.[br21.325]
4e3372ce_title: "Try to have another nurse with you when you prepare a high-risk medication like insulin or heparin."
cf0bc6f1_text: This is great advice. Especially when administering a high-risk medication like insulin or heparin,[br6.363][br6.444]it never hurts to double-check with a second nurse to ensure a situation like this one doesn't happen.[br12.49][br12.571]It's important to check your facility and state policies on all high-risk medication administration.[br18.068]
516d9597_title: "Check a client's blood glucose level before you administer insulin."
e1f482d2_text: This is the most important advice to give. If Vic had checked Rhea's blood glucose level in the first place,[br6.357][br6.438]it's very possible this whole situation could have been avoided.[br9.615]
ae4cb294_title: "When preparing and administering medication, it's important to anticipate and prevent any threat to client safety."
4711038c_text: Client safety is of utmost concern to nurses. Knowing the risks associated with insulin,[br6.658][br6.739]Vic could have anticipated how it could impact Rhea's safety and taken more steps to prevent the error.[br13.016]
de69d234_title: That's all the advice I would give.
e6cae818_text: What other advice would you give Vic, if any?[br2.531]
36761f15_text: By the start of our next shift, Rhea had completely stabilized and was safely in recovery from her procedure.[br6.976][br7.547]Vic and I checked in with Rhea's primary care provider to confirm[br11.228][br11.309]that it was time to administer another dose of insulin.[br14.279][br14.36]Then, Vic prepared the insulin, and I was there with him to make sure it was the correct amount.[br19.397]
1329ecc0_text: For a high-risk medication like insulin, check the policies where you work[br4.46][br4.541]to determine if you need confirmation of the amount before administering it.[br8.884][br9.306]I'll say in my experience this has prevented some errors. One time, I saw a student start to use the wrong syringe,[br16.371][br16.452]and they were about to administer 3 mL instead of 3 units of insulin.[br21.427][br21.508]Thankfully, I was there to prevent that error before it happened.[br25.032]
b865ed8e_text: As I said earlier, it can be stressful to think about making errors. But the truth is, we all make them sometimes.[br8.548][br8.629]We're all on the same team working to keep our clients safe, no matter what happens.[br13.403]
c47fa77a_text: In your own practice, use your clinical judgment skills to anticipate possible errors before they happen.[br7.267][br7.348]If you're not sure about something, ask. And if you do make an error, let your manager or supervisor know immediately.[br15.705][br15.786]Like I said, everyone is on the same team working to keep our clients safe. Thanks for talking with me today![br23.134]
4283f37a_text: If you want to view this feedback later, use the Print button to download a copy.
f8bf1dec_text: You talked with Rebecca (she/her), a clinical instructor, about a clinical student, Vic (he/him) she was supervising. Vic was caring for a 41-year-old client, Rhea (she/her), who had just undergone a cholecystectomy.
5388e824_text:
Before Rhea's procedure, Rhea had nothing by mouth, also known as NPO. After the cholecystectomy, Rhea returned to her room in the surgical unit in stable condition with her pain level under control. Vic briefly checked the electronic health record (EHR) and noticed that Rhea was now on a clear liquid diet and had an order for 3 units of insulin given subcutaneously.

Rebecca asked you what you would do in Vic's situation.
98623e8b_text: <indent=10%>- Recheck the orders to make sure nothing was missed.
b2a0814f_text: <indent=10%>- Recheck the orders to make sure nothing was missed.
89c0c13e_text: <indent=10%>- Make sure Rhea has received previous insulin doses.
31f9ba87_text:
Administering insulin without double-checking the EHR and knowing blood glucose levels can result in medication errors. Even after carefully verifying the EHR, it is important to consider all available information and cues, including blood glucose levels, as there may be a system error if Rhea's post-op orders were not accurately updated.

In this case, Vic made an individual error by following the initial information he saw in the EHR and administering the 3 units of insulin.
0f7712ff_text:
A few minutes later, Rebecca checked on Rhea and noticed that Rhea was confused and agitated. Rhea was sweating heavily and appeared lethargic.

Rebecca asked you to determine the most likely issue affecting Rhea.
23e93c92_text:
You correctly selected:

<indent=10%>- Rhea is probably hypoglycemic because she received insulin.

<indent=0%>If Rhea's blood glucose level was low and she received insulin, it could result in hypoglycemia. This could explain her manifestations of sweating, confusion, agitation, and lethargy.
03402689_text:
You incorrectly selected:

<indent=10%>- Rhea could be internally bleeding from the cholecystectomy.

<indent=0%>While sweating and lethargy are often associated with internal bleeding, agitation and confusion are less common.
f245cbba_text:
You incorrectly selected:

<indent=10%>- Rhea might be going into sepsis from an infection we missed.

<indent=0%>Sweating, lethargy, and confusion are typical manifestations of sepsis, but the presence of agitation does not align with this condition.
ec9b2173_text:
You incorrectly selected:

<indent=10%>- It's possible Rhea is having an allergic reaction.

<indent=0%>Allergic reactions can cause agitation, but they may not always result in lethargy, sweating, and confusion.
4d700cb7_text:
Instead, you could've selected:

<indent=10%>- Rhea is probably hypoglycemic because she received insulin.

<indent=0%>If Rhea's blood glucose level was low and she received insulin, it could result in hypoglycemia. This could explain her manifestations of sweating, confusion, agitation, and lethargy.
cb5b9461_text: Rebecca asked you what you would do first if you suspected a client was experiencing hypoglycemia.
5b996322_text:
You correctly selected:

<indent=10%>- Check the client's glucose levels.

<indent=0%>If a client may be suspected of hypoglycemia, the first step is to check their blood glucose levels and make sure they are within the expected range. If it is deemed safe, the client may be provided with carbohydrates.
ed7d8da3_text:
You incorrectly selected:

<indent=10%>- Give the client carbohydrates.

<indent=0%>Although offering Rhea clear liquid carbohydrates, such as apple juice, can be helpful, it is recommended to check her blood glucose levels beforehand to determine if these liquids are safe.
658e5b3e_text:
You incorrectly selected:

<indent=10%>- Administer more insulin.

<indent=0%>If a client might be hypoglycemic, avoid administering additional insulin as it would further reduce blood sugar levels.
068a5d06_text:
You incorrectly selected:

<indent=10%>- Check the client's blood pressure.

<indent=0%>While it is important to monitor postoperative vital signs, at that moment, the client's blood pressure was not as urgent as determining their blood glucose levels and potentially providing carbohydrates.
05f6c23f_text:
Instead, you could've selected:

<indent=10%>- Check the client's glucose levels.

<indent=0%>If a client may be suspected of hypoglycemia, the first step is to check their blood glucose levels and make sure they are within the expected range. If it is deemed safe, the client may be provided with carbohydrates.
9ff8a553_text:
In Rebecca's case, Rhea's blood glucose level was 51 mg/dL, so Rhea was provided apple juice since she was on a clear liquid diet. Rebecca then monitored and assessed Rhea over the course of 15 minutes and checked her glucose levels again to determine next steps. At the same time, Rebecca wanted to inform Rhea about the situation.

Rebecca asked you how you would explain the situation to Rhea.
2620e4d2_text: You correctly selected: - "Your blood glucose level is low, so we're giving you some carbohydrates to get it back up." This sticks to the facts and honestly tells Rhea about the issue and how it's being addressed. After 15 minutes, Rhea's blood glucose level increased to 80 mg/dL, returning to a safe range.
87be2052_text: You incorrectly selected: - "Vic gave you insulin when he shouldn't have, so now we need to stabilize your glucose level." At that time, Rebecca's priority was to stabilize the client. Once the situation is resolved, she can provide more information about the decrease in blood glucose levels. However, she intended to avoid assigning blame to Vic or any other members of the healthcare team, even during the detailed explanation.
11bc531a_text: You incorrectly selected: - "Don't panic, but you're hypoglycemic. If we can't get your blood glucose levels up, you'll really get into trouble." It is never appropriate to use scare tactics with clients as it may induce panic and have adverse effects on their health outcomes. Instead, focus on the most relevant facts.
cf220e3e_text: You incorrectly selected: - "Your blood glucose level is low, but don't worry about it. Everything will be fine." Offering false reassurance to clients is not an effective approach. It can mislead them to believe that everything is fine, even when the nurse may not have that information yet. It is better to focus on the most relevant facts.
f45b69f3_text: Instead, you could've selected: - "Your blood glucose level is low, so we're giving you some carbohydrates to get it back up." This sticks to the facts and honestly tells Rhea about the issue and how it's being addressed. After 15 minutes, Rhea's blood glucose level increased to 80 mg/dL, returning to a safe range.
369be8bf_text:
Once Rhea's condition began to stabilize, Rebecca updated the EHR with details about the drop in blood glucose, the administration of clear liquid carbohydrates, and how the blood glucose level changed afterward. She directly contacted Rhea's primary care provider and surgeon to make sure they were aware of the incident. Finally, Rebecca informed the next shift about the situation so they could monitor Rhea as needed.

Afterward, Rebecca had a conversation with Vic where she provided feedback on how to prevent similar errors in the future.

Rebecca asked you what instructions you would give Vic.
3b29ebb2_text: - "Try to have another nurse with you when you prepare a high-risk medication like insulin or heparin."
39a8399b_text: - "Check a client's blood glucose level before you administer insulin."
fa6c9637_text: - "When preparing and administering medication, it's important to anticipate and prevent any threat to client safety."
d21c1659_text: - "Try to have another nurse with you when you prepare a high-risk medication like insulin or heparin."
6b99f42e_text: - "Check a client's blood glucose level before you administer insulin."
311d1f78_text: - "When preparing and administering medication, it's important to anticipate and prevent any threat to client safety."
4c006d1b_text:
When administering high-risk medications like insulin or heparin, it is advisable to double-check with another nurse to prevent situations like the one described. Adhere to facility and state policies regarding the administration of high-risk medications.

If Vic had initially checked Rhea's blood glucose level, it is very likely that this entire situation could have been avoided.

Nurses prioritize client safety above all else. Understanding the risks associated with insulin, Vic could have anticipated its potential impact on Rhea's well-being and taken additional precautions to prevent the error.

The EHR should be regarded as a valuable tool. Instead, of telling Vic not to trust the EHR, he should be reminded to verify orders in the EHR, particularly if they appear to pose a risk to the client. If necessary, orders can always be confirmed with the provider.
aa5526a8_text:
By the start of the next shift, Rhea had completely stabilized and was safely in recovery from her procedure. Rebecca and Vic spoke to Rhea's primary care provider to confirm it was time for another insulin dose. Vic prepared the insulin while Rebecca made sure it was the correct amount.

You were asked to take on Vic's perspective and deliver the correct dosage of insulin.

5a49a2dd_text: You incorrectly pumped 1 unit of insulin.
b269b2ab_text: You incorrectly pumped $value(var_insulin_attempt_1) units of insulin.
e2253b6d_text: You correctly pumped 3 units of insulin.
fe6a1fd3_text: Then you incorrectly pumped 1 unit of insulin.
e0e89b34_text: Then you incorrectly pumped $value(var_insulin_attempt_2) units of insulin.
166ce02f_text: Then you correctly pumped 3 units of insulin.
42e223c7_text: Finally, you incorrectly pumped 1 unit of insulin.
aef41570_text: Finally, you incorrectly pumped $value(var_insulin_attempt_3) units of insulin.
ca9b4e40_text: Finally, you correctly pumped 3 units of insulin.
bdd3dc6a_text: For a high-risk medication like insulin, check the policies where you work to determine if you need confirmation of the amount before administering it. This can prevent errors in dosage, like administering 3 mL instead of 3 units of insulin.
5ee4e4a7_text: Max Value does show up in reports, so set it to the highest possible raw score.
7b146467_text: Make sure Min can accommodate the lowest scoring weight and Max can accommodate the highest scoring weight.
650f7d51_text:
Necessary for calculating percent-correct scores.

Set its Value according to what will be in the menu on the first turn. If a Poor will be available, set it to your Poor score weight; if only a Weak will be available, set it to your Weak weight, etc.

Make sure the Min and Max properties can accommodate the bottom end of possible raw scores in your convo. It doesn't hurt to set them arbitrarily low/high, like -1000 and 1000.
be1e192d_text:
Necessary for calculating percent-correct scores.

Set its Value according to what will be in the menu on the first turn. If a Target will be available, set it to your Target score weight; if only a Fair will be available, set it to your Fair weight, etc.

Make sure the Min and Max properties can accommodate the top end of possible raw scores in your convo. It doesn't hurt to set them arbitrarily low/high, like -1000 and 1000.
7705fb7c_text: Assuming three performance levels. Adjust as necessary.
67df6528_text: This sets the scoring weight of Target nodes. Assumes a weight of 10; adjust the Value field above as necessary.
c0360b0f_text: This sets the scoring weight of Fair nodes. Assumes a weight of 8; adjust the Value field above as necessary.
cb917a7e_text: This sets the scoring weight of Weak nodes. Assumes a weight of 2; adjust the Value field above as necessary.
f625a5a6_text: This sets the scoring weight of Poor nodes. Assumes a weight of 0; adjust the Value field above as necessary.
aec94132_text:
The update node uses this variable to gauge whether the convo is complete, so it knows if it should jump to the dashboard.

Make sure this gets set to 1 wherever the convo ends.
9e9442ed_name: Pharmacology: Dosage Calculations and Medication Errors
